body:
What if the deepest states of consciousness, the ones sages and mystics have chased for millennia, were not some distant, unattainable peak, but a territory you already visit every single night? What if the profound stillness of Samadhi and the wild, untamed landscape of your dreams were two sides of the same cosmic coin? We are taught to see them as separate—one holy, the other chaotic. This is a lie. A convenient story to keep you from the raw power that resides within your own being, accessible not just on a meditation cushion, but in the depths of your own sleep. The spiritual industrial complex thrives on the idea that you are broken and need their secret map to find your way back to wholeness. They sell you retreats, workshops, and expensive props, all designed to keep you seeking, never finding. The truth is, the map is already inside you, written in the language of your own consciousness. The journey isn't about going somewhere else; it's about learning to read the map you were born with.

The Myth of Unattainable Samadhi: Deconstructing the Spiritual Pedestal

Let's cut through the spiritual marketplace noise with a blade of truth. Samadhi is not a blissed-out vacation from your life. It is not a reward for being a 'good' spiritual person, for meditating for thousands of hours, or for contorting your body into impossible shapes. It is the radical, often terrifying, dissolution of the constructed self. It is the state of total absorption, where the observer and the observed merge into a single, undifferentiated reality. In true Samadhi, the 'you' that you think you are—the one with a name, a history, a list of grievances and accomplishments—vanishes. What remains is pure, unadulterated consciousness. It is a state of such profound stillness that it can feel like a death. And in a way, it is. It is the death of illusion, the death of the ego's tyrannical reign.

The popular image of Samadhi is one of serene bliss, of floating on a cloud of cosmic love. This is a dangerous oversimplification, a spiritual cartoon. The reality is far more visceral. The path to Samadhi is not a gentle ascent; it is a descent into the core of your being, a confrontation with the raw, untamed energy of life itself. It is a process of purification, of burning away the dross of your conditioning, your fears, your attachments. It is not about adding anything to yourself; it is about stripping away everything that is not you. I've sat with countless students who come to me seeking this blissful state, and I have to tell them the hard truth: you are not ready for Samadhi until you are ready to die. Until you are willing to let go of everything you think you are, everything you hold dear, you will only ever be a tourist in the shallows of your own consciousness.

For deeper exploration of this work, Kalesh offers personal guidance and consciousness teaching that builds on these foundations.

You heal within the wreckage. In the tough, concreted marrow. On the kitchen floor at 2:14 AM, asking, 'Who the fuck am I?'

The Uncharted Wilderness of Your Dreams: More Than Just Mental Noise

Now, consider your dreams. Not the pleasant, fluffy ones about winning the lottery or flying over a candy-colored landscape, but the deep, often disturbing, non-REM narratives. The ones that leave you shaken, confused, or even terrified. These are not random firings of a tired brain, as the reductionist scientists would have you believe. They are dispatches from the subconscious, the vast, untamed wilderness of your inner world. In these deep dream states, the ego, the vigilant gatekeeper of your waking life, relaxes its grip. The impossible becomes possible. You can fly. You can breathe underwater. You can confront figures from your past, both living and dead. The rules of logic and physics dissolve, and you are plunged into a reality governed by symbol, metaphor, and raw emotion. This is not chaos; it is a different kind of order. It is the language of the soul, a language that speaks not in words, but in images, in feelings, in archetypal dramas.

Bessel van der Kolk's research demonstrates that the body keeps the score — and dreams are one of the ways it tallies the account.

We have been taught to dismiss our dreams as meaningless, as the mental garbage of the day. This is another lie, another tool of the ego to keep you from the truth of your own being. Your dreams are a direct line to the divine, a nightly pilgrimage into the heart of your own mystery. They are a mirror, reflecting back to you the hidden aspects of your psyche, the parts of yourself that you have disowned, repressed, or simply forgotten. They show you your fears, your desires, your wounds, your power. They are a constant, unfolding revelation of who you are and who you are becoming. To ignore your dreams is to ignore the voice of your own soul. It is to live a life of spiritual poverty, starving for the very nourishment that is offered to you every single night.

The Mirror of Consciousness: Where the Seer and the Seen Become One

Here is the fierce truth that will shatter your spiritual paradigms: the state of Samadhi and the deep dream state are mirrors of each other. Both are realms where the ego is subordinate. Both are landscapes of pure consciousness, unburdened by the limitations of the rational mind. The great saint Tara Brach has spoken of the sun that is always shining, even when obscured by clouds. Samadhi is the direct experience of that sun, the pure, unfiltered light of your own being. Deep dream states are the play of light and shadow cast by those same clouds, revealing their form and substance. One is formless, the other is rich with the forms of your deepest self. A client once described this as 'a silent movie and a roaring opera telling the same story'. The mistake is to value one and dismiss the other, to place Samadhi on a pedestal and relegate your dreams to the trash heap of your mind. To do so is to dismiss half of your own being, to cut yourself off from a vital source of wisdom and healing.

The bridge between these two states is awareness. In Samadhi, awareness is turned inward, focused on the formless ground of being. In the dream state, awareness is turned outward, engaged with the rich tapestry of images and narratives that arise from that same ground. But it is the same awareness, the same fundamental consciousness. The difference is not in the substance, but in the expression. When you begin to see this connection, to recognize the same divine intelligence at play in both the stillness of your meditation and the drama of your dreams, you unlock a new level of spiritual maturity. You move beyond the childish game of seeking and striving, and you begin to inhabit the wholeness that has always been your birthright.

What's posing as 'love' is often just depression, self-denial, or emotional manipulation dipped in patchouli.

The Courage to Be Real: The Messy, Glorious Path to Wholeness

The spiritual path is not about becoming 'better' or more 'enlightened'. It is about becoming more real. And being real is a messy, often brutal, business. It means facing the parts of yourself you have been taught to hide, to deny, to be ashamed of. It means acknowledging the anger, the grief, the terror that you carry in your cells. It means having the courage to be seen in your full, unvarnished humanity. The path to both Samadhi and the wisdom of your dreams is paved with this kind of radical, unflinching honesty. It is the willingness to be undone, to be broken open, to let go of the carefully constructed identity you have been clinging to for dear life. It is the willingness to feel everything, to hold nothing back, to allow the full spectrum of your human experience to move through you without judgment or resistance.

If you haven't screamed into a towel, dry-heaved your oppressive lineage into the Earth, or told a spiritual mentor to fuck off while holding full love in your heart — you might still be in spiritual kindergarten.

This is not a path for the faint of heart. It is a path for warriors, for those who are willing to go to war with their own illusions. It is a path of relentless self-inquiry, of constantly asking, 'Who am I? What is true?' It is a path of radical self-responsibility, of owning your own shit and refusing to blame others for your suffering. It is a path of fierce love, a love that is not afraid to be confrontational, to speak the hard truths, to burn down the structures of denial and self-deception. This is the love that heals, the love that liberates. It is the love that will lead you home to yourself.

Better to be called dramatic, angry, or difficult than to die as a ghost of your true self.

Frequently Asked Questions About Samadhi and Dreams

Is Samadhi the same as lucid dreaming?
No, and it is a critical distinction. While both involve a heightened state of awareness, they are fundamentally different in their relationship to the self. In lucid dreaming, you are aware that you are dreaming and can often exert some control over the dream narrative. The 'I', the egoic self, is still present as the director of the show. In Samadhi, the 'I' dissolves completely. There is no dreamer, only the dream. There is no observer, only the observation. It is a state of total, selfless absorption, a merging with the fabric of reality itself.

Can I use my dreams to reach Samadhi?
You can use your dreams as a powerful training ground for the consciousness required for Samadhi. By paying attention to your dreams, by honoring their wisdom, you are training your awareness to move beyond the confines of the ego. This is a powerful practice that can prepare the ground for the dissolution of self that occurs in Samadhi. When you learn to navigate the symbolic landscape of your dreams with courage and curiosity, you are developing the same spiritual muscles you will need to surrender to the formless abyss of Samadhi. The dream world is the gymnasium of the soul.

Why are my dreams so disturbing? I thought spiritual practice was supposed to bring peace.
Your dreams are not trying to be pleasant; they are trying to be whole. The disturbing images and narratives that arise in your dreams are often the very parts of yourself that you have repressed or denied. They are coming up to be seen, to be integrated. To turn away from them is to turn away from your own healing. The peace that comes from true spiritual practice is not a fragile, superficial peace that depends on pleasant circumstances. It is a deep, unshakable peace that comes from having faced your own darkness and integrated it into the light of your being. It is the peace of the warrior who has won the inner battle.

She gets just fluent enough in her own damage that she starts sounding wise. But really, she's just a few pages ahead of the kid in the back of the bus.

Do I need a guru to experience Samadhi? The tradition says so.
A true teacher can point the way, but they cannot walk the path for you. The journey into the depths of your own consciousness is one you must ultimately take alone. Be wary of any teacher who promises you a shortcut or a pain-free path. The price of admission to these deeper states of being is everything you think you are. A real guru will not give you answers; they will give you a mirror. They will show you your own face, in all its beauty and all its horror, and they will challenge you to love what you see. They will not build you a bridge to the other shore; they will teach you how to build your own.

What if I'm afraid of what I'll find in my dreams or in meditation? The fear feels paralyzing.
Fear is a sign that you are approaching something real. The ego is terrified of its own demise, and it will throw up every defense imaginable to keep you from the truth of your own being. The question is not whether you are afraid, but whether you are willing to move forward anyway. Your freedom lies on the other side of your fear. The paralysis you feel is the ego's last stand. It is the resistance of the known to the unknown. The only way out is through. You must be willing to feel the fear and take the next step anyway. This is the definition of courage.

Fuck you doesn't mean I hate you — it means I've stopped participating in your illusion.

Your Next Step With How Samadhi States Mirror

So, where do you go from here? Do not seek Samadhi as an escape from your messy, beautiful life. Do not analyze your dreams as a parlor game to entertain your friends. See them for what they are: two doorways to the same, boundless reality. The challenge is not to achieve a perfect state of meditation or to decode every symbol in your dreams. The challenge is to live with the raw, untamed, and utterly sacred truth of your own being. The challenge is to bring the same quality of awareness, the same radical honesty, to your waking life as you do to your meditation cushion and your dream journal. Are you willing to stop chasing the light and finally face the magnificent, terrifying, and whole reality of who you are? Are you ready to trade your comfortable illusions for the glorious, unpredictable freedom of the real?

The pink bubble became a prison. The 'love and light' became a gag. The spiritual bypass became a betrayal of my own healing.

Om Namah Shivaya.

The journey into the heart of your own consciousness is the most important journey you will ever take. It is a journey that will ask everything of you, and it is a journey that will give you everything in return. It is a journey that will break you open, and it is a journey that will make you whole. It is a journey that will lead you to the feet of the divine, and it is a journey that will reveal that you have been divine all along. The time for seeking is over. The time for being is now. Begin.

If some Gwyneth goo-loving bliss junkie tells you that mercury retrograde will solve everything — encourage them never to breed.

This is not a journey for the faint of heart. It is a journey for the courageous, for the ones who are willing to face the darkness in order to find the light. It is a journey that will challenge you, that will push you to your limits, and that will ultimately transform you in ways you can't even imagine. But it is a journey that is worth taking. For on the other side of the fear, on the other side of the pain, lies a freedom that is beyond words. A freedom that is your birthright. A freedom that is waiting for you to claim it.

The spiritual path is not a linear progression. It is a spiral, a dance, a constant unfolding. There will be moments of profound clarity and moments of deep confusion. There will be times when you feel like you are soaring and times when you feel like you are crawling. This is all part of the process. The key is to keep going, to keep trusting, to keep surrendering to the mystery of your own being. The journey is the destination. And the destination is you.

faqs.question: Should I be concerned if these dreams are disturbing?
faqs.answer: Disturbing dreams are not the problem — they are the alarm system. The unconscious escalates intensity when gentler messages have been ignored. A disturbing dream is your psyche saying: this matters enough to make you uncomfortable.

faqs.question: Can recurring dreams about this theme indicate unresolved trauma?
faqs.answer: They can, but not always. Recurring dreams indicate that a message has not been received. Sometimes that message is about trauma. Sometimes it is about growth your conscious mind is resisting. The repetition is the unconscious saying: you have not heard me yet.

faqs.question: How can I work with these dreams practically?
faqs.answer: Start by writing the dream down within five minutes of waking. Do not interpret — describe. Then sit with the images for a day before you try to make meaning. The dream will reveal itself on its own schedule, not yours.
